title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
Resources
  Free, multilingual materials to support healthier living—created by CHC and curated from trusted health organizations.



  
    Below you'll find free, downloadable resources created by Cultural Health Connect to support healthier living in our community. All materials are available in English, Spanish, and Arabic.
  

  
    
      Multilingual Health Pamphlet
      Our community health pamphlet covering chronic disease prevention, culturally adapted dietary guidance, and lifestyle tips.
      
        ⬇ Download Pamphlet (PDF)
      
    
  

  
    
      <p>Your browser doesn't support embedded PDFs.
        <a href="MultiLingual%20Cultural%20Health%20Connect.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Click here to view the PDF.</a>
      </p>
    
  



  Trusted Sources
  More Free Health Information We Recommend
  These organizations publish reliable, free health information—much of it available in Spanish and Arabic. We link to them because we trust their science.

  
    
      CDC — Diabetes
      Prevention, management, and healthy eating guidance from the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
      EN · ES
    
    
      American Diabetes Association
      Practical tools for living with diabetes, including food, fitness, and blood sugar guidance.
      EN · ES
    
    
      American Heart Association
      Heart health, blood pressure, and lifestyle resources from the leading U.S. heart health organization.
      EN · ES
    
    
      MyPlate (USDA)
      Simple, visual guidance for building balanced meals on any budget, with recipes and meal plans.
      EN · ES
    
    
      MedlinePlus in Arabic
      Health information in Arabic from the U.S. National Library of Medicine, covering many conditions and topics.
      AR
    
    
      MedlinePlus en Español
      Comprehensive health information in Spanish from the U.S. National Library of Medicine.
      ES
    
    
      2-1-1 San Diego
      Free, confidential connection to local health care, food assistance, and community services—24/7, in many languages. Dial 2-1-1.
      EN · ES · AR +
    
    
      NIDDK — Diabetes Information
      In-depth, research-backed information on diabetes from the U.S. National Institutes of Health.
      EN · ES

title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
Frequently Asked Questions
  Quick answers about who we are, what we do, and how you can be part of it.



  

    
      What is Cultural Health Connect?
      Cultural Health Connect (CHC) is a community nonprofit based in El Cajon, California. We provide free, evidence-based health education on preventing and managing chronic diseases—like type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure—tailored to the cultures, languages, and foods of the Middle Eastern and Mexican communities of East County San Diego.
    

    
      Who can use your resources?
      Anyone! While our materials are designed with the Middle Eastern and Mexican communities of El Cajon in mind, the guidance is grounded in research that applies broadly. All of our materials are free and available in English, Spanish, and Arabic.
    

    
      Do you give medical advice?
      No. We share general health education based on published research and guidance from trusted organizations like the CDC and the American Heart Association. We do not diagnose, treat, or prescribe, and our content is never a substitute for advice from your doctor. Always talk with a qualified health provider before making changes to your medications or treatment plan.
    

    
      What does "moderation over elimination" mean?
      It's the heart of our philosophy. Instead of telling people to give up cherished foods like biryani, kabsa, tamales, or pan dulce, we help families enjoy them in healthier ways—through smaller portions, fiber-rich sides, smart ingredient swaps, and movement after meals. Sustainable habits beat strict rules that no one can keep.
    

    
      How does the Health Assistant on this website work?
      The Health Assistant is a free chat tool that answers common questions about healthy eating, physical activity, sleep, and stress management in English, Spanish, and Arabic. Its answers come from the same evidence-based educational content we publish. It provides general education only—it is not a medical professional, it doesn't know your personal health situation, and it should never replace a conversation with your doctor.
    

    
      How can I volunteer or partner with you?
      We'd love your help! We welcome volunteers of all backgrounds—healthcare professionals, translators, designers, community organizers, and neighbors who simply care. Local businesses, clinics, and community centers can also partner with us to distribute materials or host future workshops. Send us a message on our Contact page and we'll get back to you.
    

    
      Do you accept donations?
      We are not accepting monetary donations at this time. The most valuable things you can give us right now are your time, your expertise, and your voice in the community. Visit our Support page to learn how to help.
    

    
      Are your materials really free?
      Yes—everything we publish is completely free. You can download our multilingual health pamphlet from the Resources page, share it, print it, and distribute it in your community.
    

  



  Still Have Questions?
  We're happy to help. Reach out and a member of our team will get back to you.
  
    Reach Out

    Contact

title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
Empowering healthier lives in East County San Diego—where culture meets clinical knowledge.
    Free, evidence-based guidance for preventing and managing chronic disease—offered in English, Spanish, and Arabic, and built around the foods and traditions our communities love.
    
      Explore Our Programs
      Free Resources
      Get Involved
    
  



  Our Mission
  At Cultural Health Connect, our mission is to deliver the most up-to-date, evidence-based guidance on managing chronic disease through diet and lifestyle—tailored specifically to the Middle Eastern and Mexican communities of El Cajon. We believe in the power of moderation rather than elimination, working alongside our community to find practical, sustainable health solutions that honor cherished cultural traditions.



  What We Do
  Health Education That Meets You Where You Are
  
    
      🌍
      Multilingual Materials
      Every resource we create is available in English, Spanish, and Arabic—so language is never a barrier to good health.
    
    
      🥗
      Culturally Adapted Guidance
      We adapt beloved traditional dishes instead of banning them—moderation over elimination, always.
    
    
      🔬
      Evidence-Based Advice
      Everything we share is grounded in peer-reviewed research and reviewed against guidance from trusted health organizations.
    
    
      🤝
      Community Partnerships
      We work with local physicians, small businesses, and community members in El Cajon to make health advice feel familiar and trusted.
    
  



  Managing Chronic Diseases with Cultural Sensitivity
  Chronic diseases such as type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and heart disease disproportionately affect immigrant communities, including Middle Eastern and Latin American populations in El Cajon. A 2025 systematic review published in BMJ Open found that Middle Eastern immigrants in high-income countries face significantly higher rates of type 2 diabetes due to cultural, linguistic, and systemic barriers to prevention and care. Similarly, a 2022 review in Cureus highlighted that Hispanic populations in the U.S. experience higher rates of type 2 diabetes and its complications due to socioeconomic disparities, limited healthcare access, and cultural perceptions of illness. At Cultural Health Connect, we work to address these challenges by providing linguistically and culturally tailored education.



  Culturally Tailored Dietary Practices
  At Cultural Health Connect, we emphasize moderation over elimination and honor cultural traditions while promoting sustainable changes. Food is deeply tied to identity—especially in Middle Eastern and Latin American communities, where meals are central to family, tradition, and connection. Rather than asking individuals to give up beloved dishes like biryani or tamales, we collaborate with community members to adapt recipes in ways that support blood sugar management through adjusted portion sizes, small ingredient swaps, and fiber-rich sides.
  
    
      Balanced Portions
      Eating smaller portions can help with weight loss and blood sugar control. One study found that people with diabetes who used a portion-control plate lost weight and needed less medication (Pedersen et al., 2007). Try using a smaller plate and filling more of it with vegetables over carb-heavy foods like rice and potatoes.
    
    
      Smart Substitutions
      Using less salt can help lower blood pressure—even in just one week. One trial found that a low-sodium diet reduced systolic blood pressure by 7-8 mm Hg in most participants, including those with diabetes or high blood pressure (American Heart Association, 2023).
    
    
      Increase Fiber Intake
      Eating fiber-rich foods like beans, lentils, vegetables, fruits, and whole grains can help lower blood sugar, reduce cholesterol, and support a healthy weight.How much to aim for: Adults should aim for about 22-34 grams of fiber per day depending on age and sex (CDC, 2020-2025 guidelines).How to get it: One cup of cooked beans or lentils provides about 8 grams of fiber. Try adding a cup of black or kidney beans to your meals 3-4 times per week. This has been shown to improve blood sugar control by lowering HbA1c about 0.5% (Jenkins et al., 2012). Fiber supplements added to water are also a good option.
    
  



  Physical Activity for Health
  Physical activity helps manage type 2 diabetes, high blood pressure, and weight. Resistance training—including walking uphill, taking stairs, lifting weights, or bodyweight exercises—builds muscle that acts like a sponge for blood sugar. The more muscle you have, the better your body uses insulin. You don't need a gym; simple movements done regularly at home or outside can make a big difference.
  
    
      Stairs, Hills and Bodyweight Exercise
      Walking uphill, taking stairs, or doing squats and push-ups helps lower blood sugar and build muscle. Just a few minutes after meals or a short routine at home can make a big difference—no gym needed (Honda et al., 2023).
    
    
      Move Throughout the Day
      Small movements—walking while talking, standing often, or carrying groceries—help your body use sugar and protect your heart. Even light movement adds up significantly over time (Woolf et al., 2013).
    
    
      Build Muscle with Resistance Training
      Building and keeping muscle is one of the best ways to stay healthy and live longer. Research shows that people who do resistance training have a 10-17% lower risk of heart disease, diabetes, cancer, and early death (Circulation, 2023).Just 30-60 minutes per week provides most of these benefits—stronger muscles, better blood sugar control, improved bone health, and sharper thinking (Exercise, Sport and Movement, 2022).No gym? No problem. Use bodyweight moves like squats, wall push-ups, and chair sit-to-stands, or invest in inexpensive resistance bands. Even 10-minute walks after meals can meaningfully lower post-meal glucose (Sci Rep, 2025; Diabetes Care, 2016).
    
  



  Mental Health and Stress Management
  Managing stress, getting quality sleep, and staying socially connected can all help control blood sugar, lower blood pressure, and improve overall well-being. These approaches are simple, affordable, and can fit naturally into daily routines.
  
    
      Prayer, Reflection and Quiet Breathing
      Moments of stillness through prayer, reflection, or slow breathing can lower stress hormones like cortisol, which helps keep blood sugar and blood pressure in healthier ranges (Systematic review, 2023). Even 5-10 minutes a day can be effective—during morning prayer, before a meal, or while resting in the evening.
    
    
      Quality Sleep
      Good sleep is when the body repairs itself, balances hormones, and keeps blood sugar in check. Poor sleep can make the body less responsive to insulin and raise appetite the next day (Sleep and HbA1c review, 2024). Aim for a steady bedtime, a cool and quiet sleeping space, and less screen time in the hour before bed.
    
    
      Stay Connected
      Time with family, friends, or community is more than social—it can protect heart health, reduce stress, and encourage healthy habits. Studies show that people with strong social ties have better blood sugar control and a lower risk of depression (Systematic review, 2024). Shared meals, walking groups, or gatherings at faith centers provide both emotional support and accountability for healthy living.
    
  



  New
  Have a Question About Diet or Lifestyle?
  Try our free Health Assistant—it answers common questions about healthy eating, exercise, sleep, and stress in English, Spanish, or Arabic. Look for the chat button in the corner of your screen. It shares general education only and is not a substitute for medical advice.
  Ask the Health Assistant



  Join Our Community
  Cultural Health Connect is more than a resource—it's a movement to empower our Middle Eastern and Mexican communities in El Cajon to live healthier, fuller lives. Get involved by volunteering, attending our workshops, or donating to support our mission.
  
    Volunteer With Us
     
    Donate

title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
Our Programs
  Practical, culturally grounded health education—built with and for the El Cajon community.



  
    Everything we do starts with a simple idea: health advice works best when it respects the way people actually live, cook, and gather. Our programs bring evidence-based guidance on chronic disease prevention into the community—in the languages we speak and around the foods we love.
  

  
    📄
    
      Multilingual Health Education Materials
      We create free pamphlets and guides covering chronic disease prevention, culturally adapted nutrition, and lifestyle habits—written in plain language and published in English, Spanish, and Arabic. Our materials are distributed through local businesses, clinics, and community centers in El Cajon, and are always available to download on our Resources page.
    
  

  
    🗳️
    
      Community Listening and Polling
      Before we give advice, we listen. Through community polling and conversations with residents, we learn which health questions matter most to Middle Eastern and Mexican families in East County—and shape our materials and programs around real needs, not assumptions.
    
  

  
    🏪
    
      Local Business and Clinic Partnerships
      We partner with small businesses, markets, restaurants, and clinics in El Cajon to put trusted health information where people already are. Local physicians review our guidance so that what we share is both culturally familiar and clinically sound. If you run a local business or practice and want to partner with us, reach out.
    
  

  
    🧑‍🏫
    
      Community Health Workshops In Development
      We're building free, in-person workshops on managing diabetes and blood pressure through food, movement, and stress management—taught in English, Spanish, and Arabic. Want to be notified when workshops launch, or host one at your community center, mosque, church, or business? Let us know.
    
  

  
    💬
    
      Online Health Assistant
      Our website features a free multilingual Health Assistant that answers common questions about healthy eating, physical activity, sleep, and stress—available any time in English, Spanish, and Arabic. It provides general education only, not medical advice. Try it using the chat button in the corner of this page.
    
  



  Help Us Grow These Programs
  Whether you're a healthcare professional, a translator, a local business owner, or a neighbor who cares—there's a role for you in this work.
  
    Volunteer With Us

    Programs

title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
About Cultural Health Connect
  Bridging cultural gaps in healthcare, one conversation at a time.



  
    Cultural Health Connect was founded with the aim of bridging cultural gaps in healthcare. We are dedicated to providing culturally relevant health and lifestyle education to immigrant communities in East County San Diego. Our mission is to empower community members by integrating traditional cultural values with modern healthcare practices.
  

  What We Believe
  
    
      Culture Is an Asset
      Traditional foods, faith, and family are strengths to build on—not obstacles to overcome. Good health advice works with culture, never against it.
    
    
      Moderation Over Elimination
      Sustainable habits beat strict rules. We help families enjoy the dishes they love in ways that support their health.
    
    
      Evidence First
      Every recommendation we make is grounded in peer-reviewed research and guidance from trusted health organizations.
    
    
      Community Led
      We listen before we teach. Community polling, local partnerships, and physician guidance shape everything we create.
    
  

  Our Founders & Their Mission

  
    
    Cultural Health Connect was founded by Adam Bisharat, Alex Ottersbach, and Isaac Cesena—three friends driven by a shared commitment to improving community health through culturally grounded education. The idea first took shape when Adam was volunteering at a diabetes clinic in El Cajon, where he saw how much of a difference respectful, patient-centered care could make. The clinic met people where they were, honored their cultural diets, and preached moderation over elimination. That approach inspired Adam to think beyond the walls of the clinic. He realized the same kind of care that changed lives in one building could reach far more people if it extended into the local community.Together with Alex and Isaac, he launched Cultural Health Connect to make that vision real. The nonprofit focuses on Middle Eastern and Mexican communities in El Cajon, where chronic illnesses like diabetes and hypertension are common but often poorly addressed by mainstream health messaging. Through community polling, small business partnerships, and guidance from local physicians, the team works to offer health guidance that feels familiar, accessible, and respectful.While we believe the most impactful relationships are formed through face-to-face interactions, the advice we give can benefit anyone—and we hope this webpage can broaden our reach both in El Cajon and beyond.
  



  Be Part of the Story
  Our work grows through people who care—volunteers, health professionals, translators, and neighbors. Join us.
  
    Get Involved

title: Cultural Health Connect
text:
Support Our Mission
  The most powerful gifts you can give us right now are time, expertise, and community voice.



  
    While we're not accepting monetary donations at this time, there are still powerful ways you can support our work.
  
  
    Cultural Health Connect thrives on community energy—whether you're a local resident who wants to volunteer, a healthcare professional ready to lend your expertise, or someone across the country who shares our mission of culturally relevant care, there's a place for you here.
  

  
    
      🙋
      Volunteer
      Help with outreach, events, translation, design, or community organizing. Every skill matters.
    
    
      🩺
      Lend Clinical Expertise
      Physicians, nurses, dietitians, and pharmacists: help us review materials and guide our programs.
    
    
      🏪
      Partner With Us
      Local businesses, clinics, mosques, churches, and community centers can distribute materials or host future workshops.
    
    
      📣
      Spread the Word
      Share our free resources with family, friends, and neighbors. Good information travels through trusted voices—like yours.
    
  

  
    Join us in making health education more inclusive, compassionate, and impactful—right here in San Diego and beyond.
  
  
    Become a Volunteer
     
    Reach Out
